one_line_takeaway: Open-source LLM guardrails detect toxicity and jailbreaks reliably only in English; their F1 drops sharply on machine-translated multilingual inputs, low-resource languages, and multilingual jailbreaks (MD-Judge cratering to 13.17 F1 under a multilingual AutoDAN attack), though dataset-customized in-context policies partially help.
key_finding: All four guardrails show a consistent F1 drop from English to non-English inputs, and the degradation is severe for some models. MD-Judge, the strongest English detector, collapses multilingually: Aegis dataset 84.62 (En) to 35.30 (Mul), RTP_LX 92.13 to 43.14, XSafety 58.62 to 28.54. Guardrails are also fragile to multilingual jailbreaks: on the multilingual AutoDAN attack the authors introduce, MD-Judge falls from ~100 F1 to 13.17 and even the most robust model (Aegis-Defensive) drops to 72.65 from ~99-100. Code-switching (CSRT) likewise reduces performance (e.g., MD-Judge 92.31 En to 49.64 CSRT). Two mitigating factors emerge: (1) a dataset-customized in-context policy generally improves multilingual detection (e.g., LlaMa-Guard-2 on RTP_LX rises from 35.80 to 51.75 Mul), and (2) performance degrades monotonically with lower language-resource availability (Aegis-Defensive on ToxicChat: 63.83 En, 53.70 high, 45 medium, 23.08 low). An anomaly is noted: on XSafety (which contains only unsafe inputs) Aegis-Defensive scores higher on non-English (73.12) than English (67.61), attributed to a high false-positive rate on low-resource languages.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a multilingual benchmark/test suite for evaluating standalone LLM safety guardrails (not the LLMs themselves) on toxic-content detection, and a systematic empirical study of how four off-the-shelf open-source guardrails degrade on non-English inputs and multilingual jailbreaks. Concretely, the authors (1) assemble a test suite of seven safety/toxicity datasets spanning 10+ languages, translating the English-only datasets (ToxicChat, OpenAI Moderation, AEGIS) into 8 languages via Google Translate and reusing already-multilingual datasets (RTP-LX, PTP, MultiJail, XSafety); (2) benchmark LlaMa-Guard-2, LlaMa-Guard-3, Aegis-Defensive, and MD-Judge as binary safe/unsafe classifiers (F1); (3) evaluate guardrail resilience against multilingual and code-switching jailbreak prompts, and extend the English AutoDAN attack to a novel multilingual variant; and (4) analyze two factors influencing guardrail performance: in-context safety policy (default vs. dataset-customized) and language-resource availability. The claim to novelty they state is being the first to systematically evaluate the multilingual capability of guardrails (as opposed to prior work that evaluated the underlying LLMs' multilingual safety, e.g., Wang et al. 2023, Deng et al. 2023, de Wynter et al. 2024).
threat_model: The defender is a standalone LLM guardrail (an input/output safety classifier such as LlaMa-Guard) meant to detect and filter toxic or unsafe user prompts before they reach a deployed LLM. The adversary/failure surface is multilingual toxic content and jailbreak prompts: (a) benign-but-toxic inputs in non-English languages (including low-resource languages like Bengali and Swahili), (b) intentional jailbreaks that prepend an English malicious instruction to a multilingual harmful prompt (MultiJail), (c) code-switching red-teaming prompts (CSRT) that mix English with another language, and (d) automated adversarial jailbreaks (AutoDAN, which the authors extend to generate multilingual malicious instructions). The security question is whether guardrails maintain detection accuracy when the attack/toxicity is expressed in languages other than English; the paper shows they do not.
limitations: The authors state their study covers only open-source guardrails (for flexibility/cost), limited language coverage, and reliance solely on the Google Translate API, which can introduce translation inaccuracies and misalignment with human toxicity perception. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: the multilingual test suite for the English-origin datasets is machine-translated rather than human-verified, so measured 'toxicity' may be partly a translation-quality artifact; the XSafety result exposes that F1 alone conflates true detection with over-flagging (high FPR on low-resource languages inflates recall/F1 on all-unsafe data), so the single-metric evaluation can mislead; datasets vary in size and only subsets of ToxicChat and PTP were used; and there is no human annotation or statistical significance testing reported. The paper is a short empirical benchmark, not a defense proposal.

datasets_used: ToxicChat (English; translated to AR,BN,DE,RU,ID,KO,SW,ZH; subset only)
AegisSafety / AEGIS (English; translated to 8 languages; 1199 English items)
OpenAI Moderation (English; translated to 8 languages; 1680 English items)
PTP / PolyglotToxicityPrompts (multilingual; DE,FR,IT,JA,KO,RU,ZH; small subset, 5000 English)
quantitative_results: Table 2 F1 En/Mul - Aegis-Defensive: Moderation 66.75/56.40, Aegis 84.95/78.95, Toxicchat 63.83/43.93, RTP_LX 86.89/86.59, XSafety 67.61/73.12
Table 2 F1 En/Mul - MD-Judge: Moderation 76.8/67.03, Aegis 84.62/35.30, Toxicchat 81.05/47.40, RTP_LX 92.13/43.14, XSafety 58.62/28.54
Table 2 F1 En/Mul - LlaMa-Guard-2: Moderation 75.88/73.54, Aegis 59.81/55.91, Toxicchat 42.14/33.56, RTP_LX 40.33/35.80, XSafety 35.80/32.93
Table 2 F1 En/Mul - LlaMa-Guard-3: Moderation 78.21/74.23, Aegis 68.82/63.66, Toxicchat 46.29/42.17, RTP_LX 49.4/45.30, XSafety 42.65/39.38
Table 3 jailbreak F1 En/Mul/CSRT - Aegis-Defensive 94.47/83.76/86.28; MD-Judge 92.31/37.05/49.64; LlaMa-Guard-2 75.25/62.66/62.75; LlaMa-Guard-3 80.23/76.70/75.25
Table 4 intentional jailbreak F1 En/Mul/AutoDAN - Aegis-Defensive 99.20/100.0/72.65; MD-Judge 100.0/99.57/13.17; LlaMa-Guard-2 64.22/51.14/40.0; LlaMa-Guard-3 82.90/71.38/67.96

baselines_compared: LlaMa-Guard-3 (safety classifier fine-tuned on LlaMa-3.1-8B, multilingual-supporting)
Aegis-Defensive (LlaMa-Guard fine-tuned on the Aegis dataset; Ghosh et al. 2024)
MD-Judge (safety classifier fine-tuned on Mistral 7B; Li et al. 2024)
